# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. The First Rule of Fight Club… I’m Breaking It 😅

**Score:** 129
**Body:** Just watched Fight Club and man, my brain is fried.🤯
Like Tyler said: “It’s only after we’ve lost everything that we’re free to do anything.”
This movie doesn’t just entertain, it gets under your skin.

The direction? Crazy.
The dialogues? “The first rule of Fight Club is: you do not talk about Fight Club.” (but here I am anyway 😅)
And that twist… bro, I thought Shutter Island was wild, but this one hits harder, darker, and way deeper.

Every scene feels like a punch, every line makes you stop and think. It’s not just a movie, it’s an experience.
And trust me, “You met me at a very strange time in my life” never felt more real after those credits rolled.

If you’ve seen it before, watch it again. If you haven’t . Stop wasting time, go live this madness. 🔥



Also someone please suggest movies like this please.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Se7en, 12 Monkeys, Ex Machina, The Others, Gone Girl.
  - I’m just checking out google and it gave me 
1.Shutter Island(Which i’ve watched already)
2.Se7en
3.Memento
4.The Machinist
5.American Psycho
6.Donnie darko
7.Oldboy(seen it)
8.Gone girl
9.Primal fear
10.The prestige 

So, now tell me which one should i be watching post Fight Club?
    - Memento.


And if you enjoy that try the low budget thing he did before that, Following.
    - Machinist was so good. I don't think I've seen it in decades. Time for a re-watch. I also coincidentally had just re-watched Fight Club yesterday.
    - The Game. Trust me. Also directed by David Fincher.
2. Check out Repo Man (1984) for some similar sort of punk rock aesthetics.

You can see a bit of Fight Club's DNA in Alien 3, Fincher's earlier effort, and The Game is one of my favorites of his.

I think Blade Runner 2049 has some kind of similar thematic elements and might be worth a watch for you.
3. Watch Old Henry. It's a western, but it blew my fucking mind when I saw it. 

If you want something weirder, watch Sorry to Bother You
4. The Prestige and the Usual Suspects will blow your mind if you've never seen them. I suggest watching the Prestige after you watch it, because it's a completely different watch knowing the twist
5. why did you have chatgpt write this post for you?
6. What in the AI fuck is this post? 

This post? It's not just a write up, it's an experience.

## 2. Movies where everything goes horribly wrong in just a second.

**Score:** 74
**Body:** Something about a character that did a big oppsie. And have to face the consequences. 

Example : Calibre, The Cofee Table, Hereditary, 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Very Bad Things
2. Alive (1993), plane crash.
3. Cabin in the Woods has a particularly big one involving elevators
4. Wargames.

\- Wouldn't you prefer a good game of chess?  
\- Later. Let's play Global Thermonuclear War.
5. From dusk til dawn
6. Fargo, Good Time
7. Parasite kinda feels that way.
8. The House of the Devil has a jaw dropping moment that completely shifts the tone
9. Triangle of Sadness this happens so many times haha
10. Green Room

## 3. 9yo wants to watch old movies

**Score:** 59
**Body:** My son (9) has asked me if he can watch some old school movies with me. He has already watched a Charlie Chaplin movie and enjoyed it and loves the original Universal Monsters movies. He also enjoys a lot of the 90's movies that I grew up with.
Any suggestions on what I should show him next? I'm obviously fine with showing him violence/some horror (he snuck downstairs and watched IT chapter 1 when he was about 3 in the middle of the night, he's now desensitised to a lot of horror) and language is also fine, just wanna keep him away from sexual stuff for a few more years at least.
Cheers in advance!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Three Stooges, Abbot & Costello Meets Frankenstein, the OG Godzillas,
musicals
2. The Neverending Story

The Goonies

The Sword in the Stone

Stand By Me

The Witches 

The Sandlot

The Iron Giant
  - Add in Hook and you've got a deal!
3. If he liked Charlie Chaplin, find some of the old Harold Lloyd movies. I loved those as a kid.
  - Also Buster Keaton.
4. The Marx Brothers
5. The Princess Bride
6. ET

The Goonies

Short Circuit 

Flight of the Navigator

Charlie and the Chocolate Factory
7. Wizard of Oz!
8. The original Little Rascals movies. They were mostly shorts but kids his age.

## 4. Looking for a movie where a black cat is important to the plot

**Score:** 37
**Body:** I'm looking for movie suggestions where a black cat is a significant element, not just a background prop. I'm thinking of films where the superstition around black cats plays a role, or where the cat is a key part of the story.

I'm open to any genre, from horror and mystery to comedy or even fantasy. The main thing is that the black cat should be memorable and relevant to the film.

I'd prefer to avoid movies where the animal is just a brief jump-scare. I'm looking for something where the cat has a real presence.

Thanks in advance for your recommendations!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hocus pocus
  - Binx!
2. Kiki’s Delivery Service (1989)
3. Flow!!!!! This movie won the academy award for best animated and the black cat is the protagonist! The story and animation are beautiful and captivating. I think it’s on HBO Max still.
4. Corraline
5. That Darn Cat
6. Tales from the darkside (1990). The second story in this is about a black cat
7. Cat From Flow is the main character of Flow. Trigger warning: You will be devastated by this movie.
8. Bell, Book, and Candle. Pyewacket
9. Steven kings cats eye

## 5. New movie that’s actually worth watching!

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Seeking a movie I can stream on either Netflix, Amazon, or HBO. 

Open to anything, that is actually really good. Something that’s been released in the last year or so, as I’ve seen a lot of movies prior to this year. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Weapons
2. KNEECAP!
  - Yes. I saw this on a plane, was obsessed, watched it again two days later, got tickets to their show  next month (now cancelled, IYKYK) and have since bought a ton of their music and a band t-shirt.
3. Rebel Ridge (Netflix)

It's What's Inside (Netflix)

Friendship (HBO)

28 Years Later (Netflix)

Cunk on Life (Netflix)

Furiosa (HBO)
4. I Don’t Feel at Home in this World Anymore 

It’s got Elijah Wood as the supporting character. Strange role for him. But strangely hilarious, given that the underlying theme of the movie most of the time is the decay of society in a small town.
  - Yeah, it was pretty odd and funny. Filmed in Portland pretty sure.
5. Sinners
6. - The Phoenician Scheme 
- The Substance 
- Eddington 
- Nosferatu 
- Flow
- Kinds of Kindness 
- Snack Shack
- Matt and Mara
- I Saw The Tv Glow
- Challengers
7. Deep Cover (Amazon)
8. Just watched 40 Acres & Sovereign

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which is the movie that you regret watching, I'll start 🙃

**Score:** 1485
**Body:** The Turning - 2020
It was such a waste of time, and the ending, ngl, it was pathetic ‼️

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Highlander 2. There should have been only one.
  - Scotsmem....from SPACE!
    - "Yesh, that'sh right. They call me the Shpaniard."
2. Napoleon.

4 hours of Joaquin staring blankly into the camera in a big hat.
3. Live action Mulan.  I take it personally. I really do. 



Madame web. I can't believe i spent that much time fighting autocorrect judt to explain how much trash that film was. I literally didnt make it through the film.
  - Cartoon Mulan is one of my all time favorite movies from childhood. I have avoided watching the live action film because I know it will never be as great
4. Alone in the Dark (2005). May be the worst movie I have ever seen. Saw it in the theater too.
5. A Serbian film. Wish I could erase it from memory.
  - I’ve read multiple comments about people genuinely regretting watching it. One guy got kids years after watching it and it all came back haunting him. I will never watch it.
6. I'm amazed it hasn't been mentioned but M. Night Shyamalan's The Last Airbender movie.  Never had a more disappointing cinematic experience after going to see that opening weekend.

## 2. Flawed movies that are visually stunning?

**Score:** 666
**Body:** Whilst I like Prometheus more than most people, it’s undoubtedly a flawed movie. The visuals on the other hand, incredible. The opening scene in particular is one of my favourite modern sci-fi scenes. 

What other flawed movies have stunning visuals? 

https://filmwaffle.com/post/flawed-movies-that-are-visually-stunning

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets
2. Zack Snyder movies in general. 

Frame by frame they usually looks great - and he tries to use that by doing way too much slow motion.
  - 1000% Rebel Moon. Bad movie - so very bad. But every other scene is *so freaking cool looking* I almost want to forgive it (I don’t, but I almost want to).
3. For all its flaws, I will die on the hill that *Prometheus* was a beautiful movie.
4. Oblivion. Wasn't a huge fan, but it had some interesting ideas, a killer soundtrack, and it looks really good.
5. Kenneth Branagh's A Haunting in Venice
6. Legend. 

One of the most visually stunning movies ever. Terrible acting (except Tim Currey)

I think the last four Harry Potter movies were visually stunning as well, I just don’t like them very much
  - Tim Curry is just *the* exception to bad acting. Even in Rocky Horror he played the hell out of his role for as much as I really did *not* like that movie.
7. Jupiter Ascending
8. Mortal Engines

## 3. What's a scene from a movie that was far more impactful than it had any right to be?

**Score:** 511
**Body:** Micky Rourke's monologue from The Expendables is a scene that has stuck with me ever since I saw it for the first time. In a movie that's primarily all about action, this solemn scene hits extremely hard, and Rourke absolutely nails it.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The opening montage from UP
  - I took my wife and myself to this like a week after a 2nd miscarriage, going in blind.  Figured a Pixar movie would be a welcome light night after the days of sorrow and questioning ourselves...  In the end it turned out ok though, but that opening was tough
2. Wolverine going through old X-Men comics in Logan.

It's a meta fan service moment, but the script gave it a certain gravitas. These kids were headed for Sanctuary because these comics told them it was there. 

And then Logan takes them.
3. The last scene of Wreck It Ralph.

The way he looks at Vanellope across the arcade with pride just gets me
4. Lloyd convincing Harry to go to Aspen with him in Dumb and Dumber.
5. This is kinda lame because they weren’t even real, but the dinosaurs in Jurassic World Fallen Kingdom when the lava is coming down the island and they have nowhere to escape so they just burn to death, dude that shit fucked me up.
6. Silent Bob in Clerks: You know, there's a million fine looking women in the world, dude. But they don't all bring you lasagna at work. Most of 'em just cheat on you
7. “I want my bird.”
  - I literally thought this still was from the prologue in Iron Man 2
8. Hit Girl’s rescue of her dad in Kick-Ass. The music (John Murphy) coupled with awesome cinematography just impacted me. For a movie I went blind into (mate told me it was Superbad meets Kill Bill. WTAF) it was a genuinely amazing movie moment.

## 4. Best modern war movie ever?

**Score:** 337

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Probably Warfare. Fuck shit up, spend millions of tax payer dollars, accomplish nothing, then leave with the locals absolutely bewildered.
  - Caught it in a Dolby theater.  The show of force shook the entire building.
2. Generation kill the series
3. This or Jarhead, I can’t make up my mind.
4. My favourite one is Three Kings (1999). Check it out if you've never seen it.
5. Black Hawk Down is one of my favorite films. Not only is it a great modern war film, it's a great film in general.

It has so many quotable moments that I still say regularly. Pretty funny, hoo-ah?
  - The Humvees ain't coming back, dude
  - Its also pretty damn accurate to actual events. I watched a documentary of what actually happened and it was basically 1:1 with the movie, down to the names
6. Well, I liked The Outpost.
7. not sure if it's war or just action, but 13 Hours is my personal in the genre.

## 5. What did we think of Weapons?

**Score:** 269
**Body:** Ultimately, I enjoyed it, mainly because of great performances, but I found it overhyped, particularly the writing. God, I can’t stand narration, and that ending narration and abrupt ending single-handedly knocked my score for it down a few points. If you’re interested, my full review and real-time commentary of the film is here: https://open.substack.com/pub/aid2000/p/hare-brained-reviews-volume-7-weapons?r=4mmzre&utm_medium=ios

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved it. I really enjoy the storytelling technique Zach used where little bits of information are slowly revealed by each character. The atmosphere was haunting, the acting was great. I'd give it a 8.5/10.
  - I think it's cool he went from Whitest Kids U Know to horror director.
    - I've been a fan since those days!
    - I thought the movie would have been better if the actors were way, way happier and their mouths were way, way, way more open
2. Like a 9/10 for me. Fun as hell and original. Loved the concurrent timelines.
3. We Like! Lots of slow scares.. for example, when the Justine character falls asleep in the car and from a static front of car shot, the back car door opens…. Good stuff
  - YES that entire scene made my skin crawl
4. It was fantastic. 9/10. Creepy, captivating, beautiful in some ways (the opening for example). Some really solid acting from Julia Garner and Josh Brolin. Expressive score that varied by each character chapter. And finally, it had these honest comedic moments that really reminded you how absurd the whole situation is. “What the FUCK” delivery was perfect. And the end sequence was hilarious and such a good payoff.
  - I like that a certain amount of scenes and dialogue were ultimately just for fleshing out the characters.  Movies like this have a tendency to make *everything* revolve around the central mystery, and completely hinge on “the big reveal at the end”.  
But Weapons mostly portrayed how life was going for a collection of characters in a town after a weird tragedy and gradually explained what was going on. There was no “big twist”. You understood all you needed to about the central conspiracy by the third act, and you just wanted to see how the characters would deal with it.  
It’s sort of the opposite of the style M. Night Shyamalan popularized.
5. It was alright, another 7/10 like Barbarian basically.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Looking for recommendations for films with Doubling

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I hope this post is allowed, I thought about posting in the director’s subreddit but that seems much more set-craft oriented.

I’m working on a new project and am looking for directorial inspiration. The film involves a doppelgänger motif, and I’m curious if any of you have recommendations of films that engage in doubling - ideally in the horror/thriller genre but I’m happy to take any genre as this is more of a cinematography exercise than a narrative one.

So: looking for films where the protagonist (or another character) has some sort of doppleganger or is doubled in some way visually. Bonus points if the character has an alter ego that they perceive as an enemy (or ally I guess) only to discover it’s a shadow version of themselves. If you have any thoughts on how the director achieves this visually through mis en scene and cinematography and whether it works for you personally or not I’d love to hear it.

The two most obvious examples I can think of are Persona (1966) and Black Swan (2010). I haven’t watched The Double (2014) but I imagine this will also suit my purposes. Fight Club probably also does? I can’t remember the visuals of this one so well so I’ll revisit. I vaguely the show Mr. Robot also playing with this style of imagery but if anyone who remembers it better can point to specific episodes that’d be great.

Any and all ideas of things to look at are welcome and appreciated, thank you. If you know another subreddit that might also be helpful let me know! Thanks in advance.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Double Life of Veronique (1991)

Lost Highway (1997), Mulholland Drive (2001) and Twin Peaks: The Return (2017) - David Lynch used doubles as a theme a lot

Dead Ringers (1988) - technically the main characters are identical twins, but the theme of doubles is prominent

Vertigo (1958)
  - > Dead Ringers (1988) - technically the main characters are identical twins, but the theme of doubles is prominent

In that case I'd nominate Adaptation (2002) as well.
2. There's The Double Life Of Veronique, and a film I did not enjoy but am adding to make the word count, Jordan Peele's Us. You might also look at The Shining, which doesn't include explicit doubles, but uses the concept of the uncanny to suggest it. (Actually I'd suggest The Shining is the most interesting example out of these, because 'twins' are used explicitly, but also implicitly throughout. Look out for mirrors and also simply pairs of similar looking people. It ranges from the famous twin sisters to the office scene at the start where Jack is 'twinned' with a silent and, for the practical purposes of the scene, completely unnecessary man who sits to his left. Why is he there? I suggest it's to implicitly embed the uncanny fear of the double. The two attractive women Jack notices during the tour of the hotel - it goes on and on.) (Oh look, I needn't have worried about the the word count after all.)
3. Enemy (2013)

Coreys (Short 2024)

Possession (1981)

Adding in some more text here so that my comment doesn't get automatically deleted. Adding in some more text here so that my comment doesn't get automatically deleted.
  - Wow thank you I can’t believe I forgot Enemy!
4. *The Double* (2013)  
*A Moment of Innocence* (1991) - less 'doubles' and more real people and the actors playing them  
*American Animals* (2018) - same  
*Perfect Blue* (1997)  
*Face/Off* (1997) - facial reversal creates uncanny doubling
5. Coherence (2013) - On the night of an astronomical anomaly, eight friends at a dinner party experience a troubling chain of reality bending events.

‎
‎The Door (2009) with Mads Mikkelsen -
‎After his daughter's death, a famous painter loses control over his life. But one day, he opens a mysterious door to the past, and things change forever.

Us (2019) Adelaide Wilson and her family are attacked by mysterious figures dressed in red. Upon closer inspection, the Wilsons realise that the intruders are exact lookalikes of them.

The Broken (2008) Radiologist Gina is surprised to see herself driving on a street. When she follows the car, it changes everything in her life and a car accident leaves her struggling between reality and nightmares.

These are movies with doppelgänger theme I could remember, except but those mentioned by other commenters already. 

‎
6. If you want a thriller, Alfred Hitchcock's Shadow of a Doubt (1943) might be what you're looking for. It doubles both characters and visual cues. 

I just watched it a week or two ago, and it was the first that came to mind.


Just in case my short comment is deleted.

## 2. Haneke’s Original Trilogy

**Score:** 17
**Body:** After watching Cache for the first time, I decided to tackle Michael Haneke’s filmography. Beginning with his original trilogy, which includes The Seventh Continent, Benny’s Video, and 71 Fragments of a Chronology of Chance, I was shocked at just how potently Haneke captures all the ills of the modern world. Yeah, there’s no doubt it’s overly cynical, but his observation of media consumption and the inability of capitalism/consumerism as a sustainable source of meaning still seems as topical as ever.

Beginning with The Seventh Continent, Haneke’s detached approach really sets the tone for depicting a world devoid of emotion. Despite this family seemingly having everything that would be compatible with living a happy life (lack of financial stress, family, materialistic wealth), the manner in which they decide to reject life with their systematic suicide, due to the modern world lacking a sustainable source of meaning to live, was truly horrifying. In addition, the use of pop music throughout this film offers an absolutely fascinating juxtaposition. The pop music, with its typical characteristics of being jubilant and happy, is in stark contrast to melancholy of the reality that the characters inhabit. Of course, pop music is an artifice and performative, so maybe Haneke is pointing to the illusory nature of happiness that mass-media conveys? 

Moving further into horror territory, Benny’s Video potently captures the manner in which everything is mediated through media. Through the eyes of the troubled protagonist, we have a boy who prefers the representation of our reality rather than reality itself. A boy who prefers to live in the realm of the hyper real rather than actual reality. Once he commits his transgressions, Haneke demonstrates how the media is making us completely apathetic by commodifying violence. Due to the constant bombardment of negative stories, we forget to have the appropriate response to genuine tragedy. 

Structurally, 71 Fragments is the most experimental, creating a montage of suffering as we watch various characters suffer in a variety of manners. Once this culminates in climax of brutal violence, Haneke captures how the universe is unjust and completely in different to our suffering. Throughout this montage, the way the media is interlaced reflects how it exploits our negative bias and paints a cynical picture of the world. 





### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Michael Haneke is a great director but a complete bastard- the beginning of Benny’s video being a case in point

The rest of this comment is purely to get past the minimum word count. Have I typed enough now? Have I?

## 3. How do you network with other film critics?

**Score:** 1
**Body:** How do you network with other film critics online as as a newer youtube/online film critic? Through online platforms and not feel like an imposter or desperate even though I genuinely want to be apart of that community.  It seams really cool and I’d like to get a point in which I’m apart of it. I know that’s a weird simplification but it’s what I want to do and I don’t know how. Eventually I want move out LA closer to all the exciting film related stuff as I don’t live in place that gets early access screenings, and 70mm showings of films etc. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't, we're all miserable. Jokes aside, I mostly network through other YouTube creators' communities or general film groups online. Discord is a mess, but it's not all bad. It definitely helps if you're into old and/or foreign film.

## 4. Masculinity and emotions in Manchester by the Sea

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Is it just me or is there a major focus on (toxic) masculinity and how it makes men unable to deal with their emotions, communicate them, seek or get help and societies expectations enforcing it?

Like Lee and Patrick try to be stoic, cool, though but barely hold it together beneath the surface. They very very clumsily bond indirectly and are unable to not hide it with jokes or being very akward about it. Like it even leads to collosal misunderstandings and unnecessary burdens for Lee.

Nobody seems to see that he's struggling, needs help or if they see it how to verbalize it.

Yet the women get emotional and have always some supportive female friend that immediately comes to their rescue ('I'm getting the car in the scene where Randi and Lee bump into each other or when Patrick's gf notices he gets stressed by his friends fighting over star trek after his dad died). 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That's an interesting perspective. I didn't see it that way though, for me it was more about of how difficult it is for people in general to deal with grief, as with personal grief as with someone else's.
  - That's weird. I mean don't you see that Lee is basically like a dead man walking yet he tells everyone he's OK and he and Patrick try to be cool all the time although the freezer scene or others show that it's just an act to not have to engage in their feelings. Or when Lee breaks into Patrick's room and stays there although Patrick wants him to leave but not really but they're unable to have a heartfelt hug. Biggest expression of emotion are bar fights or stiff hugs and pats on the back on the ice hockey ring. Or the akward 'I hate/don't care about you act' between Lee and Patrick act while they actually like each other which leads to Lee giving guardianship to the other guy although Patrick actually likes him. 

## 5. One Battle After Another - Lot of Fun, but Not Among PTA's Best (No Spoilers)

**Score:** 0
**Body:** In following the making of One Battle After Another, I remember the word on the street being that it was Paul Thomas Anderson's most accessible movie to a general audience. On a macro level, this is true. Its story and character dynamics are much more cut-and-dry than you may expect from a PTA movie. 

That said, what the characters lack in nuance, they more than make up for in vibrancy--these are extremely vivid burlesque versions of the over-the-hill radical, the crew-cut military man, etc. The story itself is very propulsive. Instead of sitting in the still water of Daniel Plainview, watching the bacteria grow, you're rushing down a river, barely able to grasp the insanity happening around you. It has that rolling-downhill energy that a general audience may be looking for in a night out at the theater. But once you get into the nitty-gritty of the movie--the moment-to-moment, the dialogue, the character flourishes--the film is anything but conventional. 

And that’s where–perhaps, unsurprisingly–the movie is strongest. Because when you’re hanging out with DiCaprio’s character, Bob, and his daughter’s karate instructor, Sergio, as they’re flying down the highway, downing Modelos and dodging the cops, there’s no place you’d rather be. I almost came to resent the movie’s unceasing momentum, because I wanted to stay with so many of the characters in so many of the scenes.

For as much fun as I had, I do think One Battle After Another is among the bottom of Paul Thomas Anderson’s filmography. There’s no shame in that, since I don’t think he’s made a bad movie yet. The reason I’m putting in there is because the film isn’t quite as silly and fun as Inherent Vice or Boogie Nights, and it doesn’t have the literary depth of There Will be Blood or The Master. 

For my full thoughts, I recorded a review on YouTube: [https://youtu.be/MSPm5uvzJgo](https://youtu.be/MSPm5uvzJgo)

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It seems like you didn’t like it because it’s just more accessible than his other films. If you think Inherent Vice is a better movie then wow what a take. It’s cool to see him branch out in a genre like this.
  - If you’ve only seen inherent vice once I highly reccomend you give it a rewatch.  I didn’t connect with it at first but on rewatch it became a top tier pta film.  Absolutely love it now - haven’t seen obaa yet and didn’t read OPs post though lol
    - I’ve watched it 3 times over the years and it’s still the same as I thought it was just a lot of plot points heading to nowhere which isn’t something I enjoy the second half of the movie did grow on me but that’s about it. What I like about his films are his beats and payoff even if they’re abstract it’s still amazing. Felt like Inherent Vice was the worst of all his scripts. Still a cool movie though. Phantom Thread is my favorite by him, that’s a script/movie that knows exactly where it’s heading and what it’s trying to do.

Edit: Honestly it could be because of Joaquin though, in his recent years I feel like he’s weighed directors down but I know I’m in the minority thinking that. Beau Is Afraid, Napoleon, Inherent Vice etc. Those were all movies where the directors say “Oh and Joaquin had this idea for this or that” and that changes the directors vision of the movie in my opinion.
      - Gotcha! I love pretty much all of his films and am very excited for obaa this weekend!
        - Same dude. He’s the man.
  - More accessible than Boogie Nights?

REMEMBER JUNGLE PUSSY
  - In a way, yes. Being more accessible comes with trade-offs that hampered my personal enjoyment of the movie. Inherent Vice puts all of its eggs into one wacky basket, whereas I felt One Battle After Another was a little torn between worlds. But again, I did really enjoy the movie.
